content:
The current $I$ is flowing through a loop $A B C D A$ as shown in the figure. The magnitude of the magnetic field at the centre ' $O$ ' is $x$ times

$$ \left(\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}}\right)\left(\mathrm{OD}=\mathrm{R}, \mathrm{DC}=\mathrm{R}, \angle \mathrm{AOD}=90^{\circ}\right) $$

The value of ' $x$ ' is ( $\mu_0=$ permeability of free space)
explanation:
Magnetic field due to a circular arc is $B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\theta I}{R}$

$$ \mathrm{B}_1=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{3 \pi}{2} \frac{\mathrm{I}}{\mathrm{R}} $$

$$ \mathrm{B}_2=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\pi}{2} \frac{\mathrm{I}}{2 \mathrm{R}} $$

$$ B=B_1+B_2 $$

$$ =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\pi \mathrm{I}}{2 \mathrm{R}}\left(3+\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{8 \mathrm{R}} \frac{7}{2} $$

$B=\frac{7 \mu_0 I}{16 R}$ in Upward direction

$$ \Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{7}{16} $$

content: A rod of circular cross-sectional area A and length $L$ is wound uniformly with $n$ turns of an insulated wire. If current flowing through the windings is I, the total magnetic flux produced inside windings is $\phi$. The relative permeability of the rod is ( $\mathrm{N}=$ number of turns per unit length) $\left(\mu_0=\right.$ permeability of vacuum $)$
Magnetic field at the centre of a circular loop of area ' $A$ ' is ' $B$ '. The magnetic moment of the loop is ' $x B$ '. The value of ' $x$ ' is ( $\mu_0=$ permeability of vacuum or free space)
explanation:
Magnetic field at centre of circular loop is given by,

$$ B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \Rightarrow I=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_0} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)$$

For circular loop,

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{A}=\pi \mathrm{r}^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{r} & =\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{A}}{\pi}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

Magnetic moment of the loop will be,

$$ \begin{aligned} & M=I A \\ & M=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_0} \times A \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & M=\frac{2 B A}{\mu_0} \times \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (ii)] }\\ & M=\frac{2}{\mu_0} \sqrt{\frac{A^3}{\pi}} B \\ \therefore \quad & x==\frac{2}{\mu_0} \sqrt{\frac{A^3}{\pi}} \end{aligned} $$

content: A straight wire of mass ' M ' and length 2 m is placed in a magnetic field of 2 T which is acting perpendicular to the length of the wire. When a current of 1 A flows through the wire, the wire experiences an upthrust and leviates in a magnetic field. The mass ' $M$ ' of the wire is (acceleration due to gravity $\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
explanation:
$$ \quad \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{BI} l \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\\ & \mathrm{~F}=\mathrm{Mg} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mathrm{M}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{~g}} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & M=\frac{B I l}{g} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\\ & M=\frac{2 \times 1 \times 2}{10}=0.4 \mathrm{~kg} \\ \therefore \quad & M=400 \text { gram } \end{aligned}\\ &\text { } \end{aligned} $$

content: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular coil of area ' $A$ ' is ' $B$ '. The magnetic moment of the coil is $x$ times $\left(2 B / \mu_0\right)$. The value of $x$ is ( $\mu_0=$ permeability of free space)
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Magnetic field at centre of circular coil is given by, }\\ &B=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 r} \Rightarrow I=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_0} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { For circular coil, }\\ &A=\pi r^2 \Rightarrow r=\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Magnetic moment of the coil will be, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & M=I A \\ & M=\frac{2 B r}{\mu_0} \times A \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots[\text { From (i)] }\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ M=\frac{2 B A}{\mu_0} \times \sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}} \quad \ldots[\text { From (ii) }] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & M=\frac{2 B A^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\mu_0(\pi)^{\frac{1}{2}}}=\frac{2 B}{\mu_0}\left(\frac{A^3}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \\ \because & M=\frac{2 B}{\mu_0} \cdot x \,\,\,\,\,\,\text { ...(given) }\\ \therefore & X=\left(\frac{A^3}{\pi}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}} \end{aligned} $$

content: To manufacture a solenoid of length ' $l$ ' and inductance ' $L$ ', the length of the thin wire required is (cross - sectional diameter of a solenoid is considerably less than length, $\mu_0=$ permeability of free space)
explanation:
Given:

  • Length of solenoid = $l$

  • Inductance of solenoid = $L$

  • Let the radius of solenoid = $r$ (cross-sectional diameter is much less than length, so $r \ll l$)

  • Length of thin wire required = ?

Step 1: Inductance of a solenoid

From NCERT:

Inductance of a solenoid is

$$ L = \frac{\mu_0 n^2 A l}{l} $$

where:

  • $\mu_0$ = permeability of free space

  • $n$ = number of turns per unit length

  • $A$ = cross-sectional area

  • $l$ = length of solenoid

But $A = \pi r^2$.

So,

$$ L = \frac{\mu_0 n^2 \pi r^2 l}{l} $$

Since the $l$ in numerator and denominator cancels out,

$$ L = \mu_0 n^2 \pi r^2 l $$

Step 2: Total number of turns

Let total number of turns be $N$.

Then,

$$ n = \frac{N}{l} $$

Therefore,

$$ L = \mu_0 \left(\frac{N}{l}\right)^2 \pi r^2 l $$

$$ L = \mu_0 \frac{N^2}{l^2} \pi r^2 l $$

$$ L = \mu_0 N^2 \pi r^2 / l $$

Step 3: Wire length calculation

Length of wire needed $= \text{circumference} \times \text{number of turns} = 2\pi r \times N $

Let the length of wire be $\ell_0$:

$$ \ell_0 = 2\pi r N $$

So,

$$ N = \frac{\ell_0}{2\pi r} $$

Step 4: Substitute $N$ in the inductance formula

Plug $N$ in $L = \frac{\mu_0 N^2 \pi r^2}{l}$:

$$ L = \frac{\mu_0}{l} (N^2) \pi r^2 $$

Substitute $N = \frac{\ell_0}{2\pi r}$:

$ L = \frac{\mu_0}{l} \left(\frac{\ell_0^2}{4\pi^2 r^2}\right) \pi r^2 $

$ L = \frac{\mu_0}{l} \cdot \frac{\ell_0^2}{4\pi^2 r^2} \cdot \pi r^2 $

$ L = \frac{\mu_0 \ell_0^2 \pi r^2}{4 \pi^2 r^2 l} $

$ L = \frac{\mu_0 \ell_0^2}{4\pi l} $

Step 5: Express $\ell_0$ in terms of $L$ and $l$

$ L = \frac{\mu_0 \ell_0^2}{4\pi l} $

$ \ell_0^2 = \frac{4\pi l L}{\mu_0} $

$ \therefore \ell_0 = \left[\frac{4\pi l L}{\mu_0} \right]^{1/2} $

Final Answer:

Option A is correct:

$$ \boxed{\ell_0 = \left[\frac{4\pi l L}{\mu_0}\right]^{1/2}} $$

content: The magnitude of magnetic induction at a point on the axis at a large distance ' $r$ ' from the centre of a circular coil of ' $n$ ' turns and area ' $A$ ' carrying current ' I ' is ( $\mu_0=$ permeabilty of free space)
explanation:
1. Magnetic Field on the Axis of a Circular Current Loop:

    First, let's recall the formula for the magnetic field produced by a single circular current loop of radius $R$ carrying current $I$, at a point on its axis at a distance $x$ from its centre.

    According to Biot-Savart Law, the magnitude of the magnetic field $B$ at such a point is given by:

    $$B = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$$

    Here, $\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space.

  2. For a Coil of 'n' Turns:

    If the coil has $n$ turns, the total magnetic field will be $n$ times the field produced by a single turn. So,

    $$B = \frac{\mu_0 n I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$$

  3. Applying the "Large Distance" Condition:

    The problem states that the point is at a "large distance ' $r$ ' from the centre". This means $x = r$, and $r \gg R$ (the distance is much greater than the radius of the coil).

    Under this condition, we can neglect $R^2$ in comparison to $r^2$ in the denominator.

    So, $(R^2 + r^2)^{3/2} \approx (r^2)^{3/2} = r^3$.

  4. Substituting the Approximation:

    Now, substitute $x=r$ and the approximation into the formula:

    $$B_{\text{axis}} = \frac{\mu_0 n I R^2}{2 r^3}$$

  5. Introducing Area 'A':

    The problem states the area of the coil is ' $A$ '. For a circular coil, the area is given by $A = \pi R^2$.

    From this, we can express $R^2$ as $R^2 = \frac{A}{\pi}$.

  6. Final Expression:

    Substitute $R^2 = \frac{A}{\pi}$ into the equation for $B_{\text{axis}}$:

    $$B_{\text{axis}} = \frac{\mu_0 n I \left(\frac{A}{\pi}\right)}{2 r^3}$$

    $$B_{\text{axis}} = \frac{\mu_0 n I A}{2 \pi r^3}$$

    To match the options, we can multiply the numerator and denominator by 2:

    $$B_{\text{axis}} = \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 n I A}{r^3}$$

Comparing this result with the given options, we find that it matches Option B.

The final answer is $\boxed{\text{Option B}}$.

content: A circular coil carrying current ' $I$ ' has radius ' $R$ ' and magnetic field at the centre is ' $B$ '. At what distance from the centre along the axis of the same coil, the magnetic field will be $\frac{\mathrm{B}}{8}$ ?
explanation:
Let us proceed step by step:

Step 1: Magnetic field at the centre of the coil

For a circular coil of radius $R$, with $n$ turns and carrying current $I$, the magnetic field at the centre ($B$) is:

$ B = \frac{\mu_0 n I}{2R} $

For this question, assume $n = 1$ (single turn coil).

Step 2: Magnetic field on the axis at distance $x$ from the centre

The magnetic field at a point on the axis at distance $x$ from the centre is:

$ B_x = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} $

At the centre ($x=0$):

$ B_0 = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} $

Given: $B_0 = B$

Step 3: Equate the field at distance $x$ with $\frac{B}{8}$

Set $B_x = \frac{B}{8}$:

$ \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{1}{8} \times \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} $

Step 4: Solve for $x$

Multiply both sides by $8$:

$ 8 \cdot \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} $

$ \frac{4 \mu_0 I R^2}{(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} $

Now, cross-multiplying:

$ 4 R^2 \cdot 2R = (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2} $

$ 8R^3 = (R^2 + x^2)^{3/2} $

Now, take both sides to the power $2/3$:

$ (8R^3)^{2/3} = R^2 + x^2 $

$8^{2/3} = (2^3)^{2/3} = 2^{2} = 4$

So,

$ 4R^2 = R^2 + x^2 $

$ x^2 = 4R^2 - R^2 = 3R^2 $

$ x = R\sqrt{3} $

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{R \sqrt{3}} $

So, the correct option is Option B: $R \sqrt{3}$.

content: A charged particle is subjected to acceleration in a cyclotron which consists of two dees ' $\mathrm{D}_1$ ' and ' $D_2$ '. The charged particle undergoes increase in its speed.

options.content: inside $\mathrm{D}_1, \mathrm{D}_2$ and the gap between dees.
only in the gap between $\mathrm{D}_1$ and $\mathrm{D}_2$.

explanation:
The charged particle gains speed only when it crosses the gap, because only there it “sees” an electric field.

• Inside each Dee (D₁ or D₂):

– There is only a magnetic field perpendicular to the velocity.

– Magnetic force is always perpendicular to motion ⇒ does no work ⇒ speed remains constant.

• In the gap between D₁ and D₂:

– An oscillating electric field exists (due to the alternating potential on the dees).

– Electric force is along the direction of motion ⇒ does work ⇒ increases the particle’s kinetic energy (and hence its speed).

Answer: Option C.

content: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular coil of area ' $A$ ' is ' $B$ '. The magnetic moment of the coil is ( $\mu_0=$. permeability of free space)
explanation:
First, the magnetic moment $M$ equals the current $i$ times the coil’s area $A$, and a circular area is $A = \pi R^2$.

$$M = i A\quad\text{and}\quad A = \pi R^2$$

Next, the magnetic field at the center of the coil is

$$B = \frac{\mu_0 i}{2 R}$$

Rearrange this to solve for the current $i$:

$$i = \frac{2 B R}{\mu_0}$$

Since $R = \sqrt{\tfrac{A}{\pi}}$, substitute into the expression for $i$:

$$i = \frac{2 B}{\mu_0}\,\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}$$

Finally, the magnetic moment is $m = iA$. Substituting $i$ gives

$$m = \frac{2 B}{\mu_0}\,\sqrt{\frac{A}{\pi}}\times A \;=\;\frac{2 B \sqrt{A^3}}{\mu_0 \sqrt{\pi}}$$
